evidence: GoogleTokenVerifier creates GoogleIdTokenVerifier in its constructor using @Value-injected clientId — this performs HTTP client setup at bean construction time, but GoogleIdTokenVerifier.Builder is lightweight and doesn't make network calls until verify() is called.

MicrosoftTokenVerifier.buildDecoder() calls NimbusJwtDecoder.withJwkSetUri(...).build() at constructor time — NimbusJwtDecoder with JWK URI fetches keys lazily on first use, not at construction. No network call at init time.
IdentityContractGuard uses @PostConstruct to validate config — clean startup validation pattern, no hidden ordering dependency.
RedisAuthCodeStore reads rolloutProperties at method execution time, not at construction — no import-time side effects.
SecurityConfig.filterChain() is a @Bean method, called at context initialization — standard Spring pattern.
No environment variable reads at class load time (static initializers) detected.
No circular init dependencies found — Spring wires all dependencies through constructor injection cleanly.
AuthRolloutProperties uses @ConfigurationProperties — values are bound after context startup, not at class load time.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: The codebase uses constructor injection throughout with no module-level side effects at import time. The @PostConstruct startup validation in IdentityContractGuard is a clean pattern. No global singleton ordering concerns or environment reads at class load time.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Score 82: Clean initialization with no hidden ordering dependencies or import-time I/O. The only minor concern is that GoogleTokenVerifier creates a somewhat heavy GoogleIdTokenVerifier object in the constructor, though it does not make network calls until use.

add.header: Constructor injection prevents initialization ordering issues
add.description: All Spring beans use constructor injection — Spring guarantees dependency resolution order, eliminating hidden initialization coupling.
